dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain.
Hello, I'm EMT Alex, and we're going to be helping you today.
I have chest pain that started about 20 minutes ago.
Can you open your eyes for me and follow a finger?
Yes.
May I ask you to squeeze my fingers? Great.
Let me just document that you are alert and oriented.
Also, you are ambulating and in no obvious distress. No life threats noted.
Thanks for confirming. Can you describe the chest pain in more detail?
It's a sharp, heavy chest pain in the front.
How severe is the pain right now on a scale of 1 to 10?
It's a 10-very sharp and heavy.
Is there anything that makes it better or worse?
No, it stays the same.
Are there any other symptoms you're experiencing alongside the pain?
Just the pain and shortness of breath.
Let's take your vital signs now. Initial blood pressure is 157 over 106.
Your heart rate is 128, which is quite fast. Let's keep this monitored.
Your respiratory rate is 13, and your oxygen saturation is at 100 percent.
Your blood glucose is 0 at this time, but we will check it again.
Your airway is clear. Do you have any trouble breathing or throat obstruction?
Respirations are regular in depth, with no signs of cyanosis or tracheal deviation.
I hear clear breath sounds on both sides and no signs of work of breathing.
Your systolic pressure is elevated, but your pulses-are they strong or weak?
Strong and regular.
Skin is pink in color and dry. No signs of coolness or pallor.
Do you have any history related to your heart or past procedures?
Yes, I had a heart attack in April 2018, and they placed a stent.
Are there any current medications you are taking or any known allergies?
I take aspirin daily. I'm allergic to penicillin.
What did you last eat or drink before the pain started?
I can't recall eating or drinking anything recently.
Your head and neck exams appear normal. No DCAPBTLS.
No signs of jugular venous distension or tracheal deviation.
Your neck veins are normal, and your pupils are equal and reactive.
And no masses, tenderness, or lymphadenopathy in the neck.
Chest has equal rise and fall with no visible deformities. 9 out of 10 anterior chest pain.
Your lungs are clear in all lobes with no wheezing or crackles.
No DCAPBTLS on the chest or torso.
Your skin is warm, pink, and dry. No rashes or bite marks noted.
No signs of discoloration or unusual growths.
Peripheral pulses are strong and regular. No wounds or deformities in extremities.
All extremities are full range of motion and sensation intact.
Starting with a 4-lead ECG monitoring now for baseline.
A 12-lead EKG shows moderate ST depression, consistent with possible cardiac stress.
I will now give you another 324 mg of aspirin as part of cardiac care.
Rechecking blood pressure now. It's 142 over 94 and trending lower.
Your heart rate has improved to 97 with sinus tachycardia still present.
Your respiratory rate is 18 and your breath sounds remain normal.
Your blood glucose is now at 102 mg/dL, up from zero. Good improvement.
We are now going to move you onto the stretcher safely. Make sure you stay as you are.
Based on our findings and your history, we're transporting you to VCU Health.
You are now securely fastened to the stretcher with all restraints in place.
Your EKG remains stable with no new changes. Still shows sinus tachycardia.
Blood pressure is 154 over 76 and oxygen saturation is 99 percent.
Respiratory rate is 20, with continued equal rise and fall of the chest.
Your pulse is 105 and stable within the tachycardic range.
Has your pain level changed at all since we began care?
Still a 10, unchanged.
We are following protocol 2-1 for suspected cardiac chest pain due to findings and history.
We're preparing for smooth handoff and communication to the receiving ED.
Again, we will transfer you to the ambulance with proper restraints and monitoring on.
No signs of distress during transport. The patient remains stable.
Patient shows no change and is still alert, oriented, and breathing normally.
Final check before transport-your blood sugar is still at 102. Great ongoing response.
ED is aware of all vital signs, history, and EKG findings for their care on arrival.
Moving you now into the ambulance with all lines in place for a safe transport.
We will monitor you throughout the entire ride until you're safely at the hospital.
VCU has been selected for his case due to appropriate facilities and specialty care.
Final vital signs are blood pressure at 154/76, heart rate at 105, and oxygen at 99 percent.
Final EKG still showing sinustachycardia-no new changes or recent acute ST elevation.
ED is alerted of the presence of significant ST depression. Continue with cardiac protocol upon arrival.
Before we complete care, you were self-administering aspirin before EMS arrived, right?
Yes, I put 324 mg under my tongue, but removed it before ambulance called.
We've re-administered 324 mg oral aspirin and documented response as unchanged.
Care plan is complete per protocol 2-1 for suspected cardiac chest pain.
Transfer to ED stretcher is finalized and patient is secured with full restraints.
All patient vitals and care history are accessible to ED reception with proper documentation attached.
Patient moved smoothly and steadily under continuous monitoring.
Arriving at VCU within 20 minutes, and ED has been prepared for immediate care.
ED bed has been cleared and we are ready for handoff at their receiving bay.
No pain progress during transport and patient has maintained stable condition throughout.
Patient remains on 4-lead ECG, oxygen on board, and IV ready if needed.
Patient sleeping but responsive on stretch-excellently behaved and compliant for transport.
Patient has no movement complaints and is alert for ED intake personnel.
Final signatures on board and all patient documentation is complete and ready for rise.
ED nurse is currently on standby for patient-handed transfer as soon as wheels are off.
Once inside, ED team will be responsible for further assessments and emergency care.
No patient deviations or drop in condition during transport-very consistent.
All emergency information and history of past procedures have been passed.
Aspirin, vitals, and EKG results are all included in printable reports and shared with ED.
Patient compliant with restraints and not in pain throughout duration.
Full acknowledgment from ED team, and mission complete.
Thank you for the cooperation and trust. We祝愿您早日康复！
Safe, smooth, and successful event concluded.
EMT team remains on standby if any additional support by hospital is needed.
Pre-hospital care documentation available for hospital record review at anytime.
Patient satisfaction post-care is reported as high and no verbal complaints noted.
All documentation doublesupportable and appendable for medical record audit.
Transfer completed, system marked clean from record.
